9752:20357.name: Radio Button
9752:20357.description:
Single-select radio button. Group radios by sharing the same `name` attribute.

## Accessibility
- Role: radio (native `<input type="radio">`)
- Accessible name: required `aria-label` prop, or wrap in `<label>`
- Group: same `name` attribute groups radios; only one selectable
- Keyboard: Space selects; Arrow keys move within group (native behavior)
- Focus: 1.5px outline, --border-secondary-component-focus, 4px offset
- States exposed: native checked, disabled
- Touch target: 16×16 visible — extend hit area via parent label or padding for the use case

4445:96840.name: Checkbox
4445:96840.description:
Standard checkbox backed by a native input. Supports checked, unchecked, and indeterminate states.

## Accessibility
- Role: checkbox (native `<input type="checkbox">`, role implicit)
- Accessible name: required `aria-label` prop, or wrap in `<label>`
- Keyboard: Space toggles
- Focus: 1.5px outline, --border-secondary-component-focus, 4px offset
- States exposed: native checked, indeterminate (set imperatively), disabled
- Touch target: 16×16 visible — extend hit area via parent label or padding for the use case

1956:29506.name: Toggle
1956:29506.description:
A two-state on/off switch backed by a native input.

## Accessibility
- Role: switch (`role="switch"`)
- Accessible name: required `aria-label` prop
- Keyboard: Space toggles; Enter toggles in most browsers (native input behavior)
- Focus: 1.5px outline, --border-secondary-component-focus, 4px offset
- States exposed: aria-checked (true / false), aria-disabled when disabled
- Touch target: 32×20 visible — extend hit area on mobile via parent container if needed for the use case

2887:26272.name: Mobile Button
2887:26272.description:
Primary 
Usage: The single most important action on a page or modal.
Behavior: Only one per view. Use for "Save," "Submit," or "Confirm."
Secondary
Usage: Supporting actions that accompany the primary goal
Behavior: Used for "Edit," or "Back."
Ghost 
Usage: Contextual actions that shouldn't distract from the main layout.
Behavior: Ideal for modal "X" close buttons or actions inside cards, list items etc
Dashed 
Usage: Explicitly for "creation" or "attachment" workflows.
Behavior: Use for "Add New Entity," "Upload File," or "Add Row."
FAB
Usage: High-frequency utility actions that persist during scroll
Behavior: Reserved for "AI Chatbot" or "Customer Support" triggers.
Destructive 
Usage: Final, irreversible actions.
Behavior: Use for "Delete User" or "Wipe Data." Usually requires a confirmation step.
Ghost Error 
Usage: Dangerous actions that are not the primary focus of the current screen.
Behavior: Use when a "Delete" option sits alongside several other secondary actions and shouldn't dominate the visual field.
Plain 
Usage: Lowest priority actions or inline navigation.
Behavior: Use for "Learn More," "View Details," or secondary navigation links.
Usage: Lowest priority actions or inline navigation.
Success
Usage: Positive, high-confidence actions that finalize a successful state.
Behavior: Use for "Approve," "Confirm Payment," or "Resolve." Avoid using this as a general "Submit" button unless the action is explicitly celebratory or affirmative.

## Accessibility
- Role: button (native `<button>`)
- Accessible name:
  - With label: `children` (the visible text)
  - Icon Only: required `aria-label`
- Keyboard: Enter and Space activate (native button)
- Focus: 1.5px outline, --border-secondary-component-focus, 4px offset (`:focus-visible`); mobile-specific Focus state pending in design
- States exposed: native disabled (prevents activation; FAB has no Disabled state)
- Sizing rationale:
  - Small (32×32) is intentionally compact for dense mobile UI (transit / rideshare-style toolbars, list-row actions). 44×44 is a guideline, not a hard requirement.
  - Medium (40×40), Large (44×44), FAB (56×56) cover standard mobile touch contexts.
- Variants without spec at idle:
  - Plain: relies on text + underline for affordance
  - Ghost / Ghost Error idle: no border at rest — context must provide clickability cues

28:38245.name: Desktop Button
28:38245.description:
Primary 
Usage: The single most important action on a page or modal.
Behavior: Only one per view. Use for "Save," "Submit," or "Confirm."

Secondary
Usage: Supporting actions that accompany the primary goal
Behavior: Used for "Edit," or "Back."

Ghost 
Usage: Contextual actions that shouldn't distract from the main layout.
Behavior: Ideal for modal "X" close buttons or actions inside floating toolbars.

Dashed 
Usage: Explicitly for "creation" or "attachment" workflows.
Behavior: Use for "Add New Entity," "Upload File," or "Add Row."

FAB
Usage: High-frequency utility actions that persist during scroll
Behavior: Reserved for "AI Chatbot" or "Customer Support" triggers.
Destructive 
Usage: Final, irreversible actions.
Behavior: Use for "Delete User" or "Wipe Data." Usually requires a confirmation step.

Ghost Error 
Usage: Dangerous actions that are not the primary focus of the current screen.
Behavior: Use when a "Delete" option sits alongside several other secondary actions and shouldn't dominate the visual field.

Plain 
Usage: Lowest priority actions or inline navigation.
Behavior: Use for "Learn More," "View Details," or secondary navigation links.
Usage: Lowest priority actions or inline navigation.

## Accessibility
- Role: button (native `<button>`)
- Accessible name:
  - With label: `children` (the visible text)
  - Icon Only / Prominent Icon Only: required `aria-label`
- Keyboard: Enter and Space activate (native button)
- Focus: 1.5px outline, --border-secondary-component-focus, 4px offset (`:focus-visible`)
- States exposed: native disabled (prevents activation; FAB has no Disabled state)
- Sizing rationale:
  - Small (32×32) is intentionally compact for dense UI contexts (toolbars, table actions, command palettes). 44×44 is a guideline, not a hard requirement.
  - Medium (40×40), Large (48×48), FAB (56×56) cover standard touch contexts.
- Variants without spec at idle:
  - Plain: relies on text + underline for affordance
  - Ghost / Ghost Error idle: no border at rest — context must provide clickability cues

8398:6917.name: Leta Logo
8398:6917.description:
LETA brand mark. Symbol (24/56/120) and Wordmark variants.

## Accessibility
- Role: img (`role="img"`)
- Accessible name: hardcoded "LETA" via aria-label
- Interactive: no — purely visual brand asset
- Decorative usage: when nested inside an interactive parent (e.g. CollapsedSidebarLogo), set aria-hidden="true" to avoid double-announcement

7900:163823.name: Map Zoom controls
7900:163823.description: Used for controlling Map zoom actions

9132:34635.name: Collapsed Sidebar Logo
9132:34635.description:
This component illustrates the behavior of the LETA logo within the Collapsed Sidebar upon hover.

When the user hovers over the logo, it transforms into the sidebar icon button. This interaction enables the user to expand the sidebar component.

## Accessibility
- Role: button (native `<button>`)
- Accessible name: required `aria-label` prop (suggested: "Expand sidebar")
- Keyboard: Enter and Space activate; Tab focuses the whole 40×40 region
- Focus: 1.5px outline, --border-secondary-component-focus, 4px offset; combined with the visual cross-fade to the Hover state
- States exposed: none beyond native button (no Disabled — sidebar always expandable when collapsed)
- Touch target: 40×40 — desktop sidebar context
- Brand-as-control: SR users perceive this purely as the expand button, never as a logo. The hover swap is visual only; don't rely on it for affordance documentation.

8269:29237.name: Mobile Chips
8269:29237.description: Chips are compact, pill-shaped element that represents a specific piece of information, an attribute, or a discrete action. They help users filter content specifically for stacked lists, make selections, or trigger context-specific tasks

8967:38281.name: Featured Icon
8967:38281.description: Visual anchors for card title icons

7139:53343.name: Desktop Chips
7139:53343.description: Chips are compact, pill-shaped element that represents a specific piece of information, an attribute, or a discrete action. They help users filter content specifically stacked lists, make selections, or trigger context-specific tasks.

5778:229946.name: Segment Switcher Tab
5778:229946.description: Base component for Segment Switcher molecule

5778:229942.name: View Switcher Tab
5778:229942.description: Base component for View Switcher molecule

3132:23448.name: Map Icon
3132:23448.description: These map icons act as the base component for all maps, pins and badges that could appear on a LETA map interface

3129:23003.name: Delivery Map Icons
3129:23003.description: These map icons are instances of the Map Icon Type component that's being used across different maps on both Desktop & Mobile

5540:68674.name: Icon/Depot-Outline
5540:68674.description: Used to represent stores, depots & customers

28:37842.name: Icon/Depot
28:37842.description: Used to represent stores, depots & customers

7751:28982.name: Tags
7751:28982.description:
Tag (Non removable)Usage: Used to categorize content, label entities with keywords, or group related items. Unlike badges, tags represent a "link" or a "searchable attribute."
Interactivity: Clicking a tag typically navigates the user to a filtered view or a specific category page.


Tag (Removable)Usage: In some contexts, tags can include a trailing "X" icon (similar to chips) if the user is assigning them to an entity.
Interactivity: Clicking the "X" removes the tag from the entity (Action).

4445:107943.name: Duration Labels
4445:107943.description: Duration Labels — a compact inline label that communicates a duration and its status, used inside table Duration cells and timing summaries. Finished pairs a status icon with greyed-out time for a completed duration (green Check-Circle = on target, red Cancel-Circle = delayed). Active shows a live, running time coloured by status (default = on target, orange = at risk, red = delayed). The time text is caller-supplied (e.g. "1h 24m 12s").

4444:45000.name: Cell
4444:45000.description:
Cell — the building block of LETA data tables. Each Cell renders one column's value for one row inside a fixed-height, padded container that carries the table's interaction states (Idle, Hover, Pressed, Selected). Header cells (40px tall) label and sort columns; body cells (72px) hold row data. The Type prop chooses what the cell renders, from a plain value to rich composite content.


Types
Header — a column-title label with optional leading/trailing info icons for tooltips.
Header Checkbox — the select-all checkbox in the header row.
Sample — a plain text value. Right-align numeric values (quantities, amounts) so decimal points and place values line up for easy scanning.
Date — a two-line date / time value.
Text Link — an inline underlined link (email, URL).
Status — a delivery / status badge (slot — can hold one or more badges).
Default Checkbox — the per-row selection checkbox.
Actions — a row-level action slot; can hold one or more icon buttons (e.g. More, Delete) or a toggle.
Preview Chips — a read-only row of chips previewing tags or categories.
Duration — a Duration Label showing elapsed/target time: a Finished duration pairs a status icon with greyed time; a live timer colours the time by status.
Select Field — an inline Select dropdown for editing the value in place.
Item Stepper — an inline numeric stepper for editing a quantity in place.
Time Stepper — an inline multi-row time editor (Hours / Minutes / Seconds steppers + Add Row).
List Item — a two-line title + description. Both truncate with an ellipsis when they exceed the column width.
Address Cell — a pickup → dropoff address pair with origin/destination icons. Each line truncates with an ellipsis.
Manual Order — a manually-created order's ID (truncates with ellipsis, max 250 characters) above a row of interactive elements — a copy-ID button plus tooltip icons telling the dispatcher more about the order (created manually, scheduled, etc.).
Automatic Order — an integration-created order: an order ID (with optional info icon and badges) above interactive elements — a copy-ID button, an integration icon, and a scheduled (calendar) icon. The interactive row gives the dispatcher at-a-glance context (copy the ID; tooltips for scheduled, manual-vs-integration, auto-broadcast).
Driver Cell — an avatar + driver name above inline action buttons (swap driver, call).


States — Idle, Hover, Pressed, Selected. Selected highlights the cell with the secondary selection surface. Header types have no Selected state.


When to use — as the cell renderer inside a data table / list row.
When not to use — standalone content blocks → Content Card; calendar day cells → the day-cell Menu Option; selectable choice tiles → Option Card.

9469:84304.name: Top Filter Section
9469:84304.description:
The Top Filter Section is the filter bar that holds a horizontal row of Top Filter controls in a table toolbar.

Usage: As the status-filter row above or within a data table. Filters are independent — several can be applied at once.
When not to use: a single filter (use Top Filter directly); navigation (use Page Tabs Control); view switching (use Desktop Segment Control).

3907:71981.name: Page Tabs Control
3907:71981.description:
Page Tabs Control is a full-width horizontal tab bar for top-level navigation between sections or pages, with a demarcator line beneath the tabs. The active tab's 2px indicator sits on the demarcator.

Usage: Basic — navigate between page sections. Wizard — a sequential multi-step flow where each tab carries a numbered step circle (future steps shown inactive).
When not to use: switching content layout (use Desktop Segment Control); filtering (use Top Filter); very large tab counts needing overflow scroll.

5778:230039.name: Desktop Segment Control
5778:230039.description:
View Switcher (Small/Compact)

Usage: Switching the layout of the current content without changing the data (e.g., Grid vs. List).
Hierarchy: Lowest level. It should sit near the content it controls, typically in a toolbar or the corner of a data section.
Behavior: Immediate toggle; no page refresh.

Section Switcher (Large/Prominent)Usage: High-level navigation within a container (like a Drawer or Sidebar). It sits below Page Tabs Control component but above the content.
Visual: Larger height (40px-48px), full-width or substantial horizontal footprint, clearly defined "selected" state with a background "pill" or "slide" animation.
Hierarchy: Mid-level. It organizes the "chapters" of a specific panel.
Behavior: Swaps out the entire body content of the drawer or panel.

1572:12741.name: Top Filter
1572:12741.description:
A Top Filter is a compact, rounded filter control used in a table toolbar to filter the table's content by status. It toggles a status filter on or off; the Advanced type reveals its status options in a dropdown.

Usage: Only for filtering the status of content within a table. Basic = a single on/off status filter; Advanced = status options revealed behind a dropdown.
When not to use: navigation, search, or as a general-purpose chip.

8567:15702.name: Page Title
8567:15702.description: Used for page headings within mobile User Interfaces with a minimum height of 32px

3061:15796.name: Section Heading
3061:15796.description: Used for tab and body section headings within mobile User Interfaces with a minimum height of 32px

3976:62589.name: CTA Section
3976:62589.description:
Horizontal for quick, low-stakes decisions
Vertical for important, consequential choices
Vertical when button copy gets long

2978:15121.name: Option Card
2978:15121.description: Used within pages and mobile trays to display a list of options, actions or features available to a user

1531:5056.name: Desktop Menu Options
1531:5056.description:
A single row inside a desktop menu, dropdown, sidebar, calendar, or pager — the basic building block of the things people pick from. Each row shows a label plus, depending on its Type, an optional leading icon, a trailing checkmark / chevron / keyboard shortcut / badge, or a built-in checkbox or radio button.

You rarely use a row on its own — stack several inside a Desktop Dropdowns panel (or a sidebar / calendar) to build the full menu.

When to use: for the individual items of any menu, dropdown, select, filter list, calendar, pager, sidebar, or side tab.
When not to use: as a standalone button (use Button) or as a top-level page tab (use Page Tabs Control).

Types
• Combobox — a selectable option in a single-select list; the chosen one gets a light highlight + a trailing checkmark.
• Dropdown Basic — a standard menu command; can carry a trailing chevron (submenu), a keyboard shortcut, or a badge.
• Dropdown-Advanced — a richer two-line item (title + description) for results or entities that need extra detail.
• Dropdown-Destructive — a dangerous command such as Delete, shown in red.
• Checkbox Selection / Radio Button Selection — rows with a built-in checkbox (pick several) or radio (pick one).
• Day Cell / Pagination — small 32×32 cells for calendar days and page numbers; the current one is filled dark.
• Time Picker — a compact time option such as "12:00 AM".
• Filter Group — a bordered filter heading; once options are ticked it shows a single "N selected" chip whose × clears them all.
• Side Bar Main / Side Bar Main (Icon-Only) / Side Bar Sub / Side Tab navigation — items for the left sidebar and side tabs; the current section is highlighted and semibold.

8338:35033.name: Mobile Menu Options
8338:35033.description:
The touch version of a single menu / list row for phones. Like Desktop Menu Options but simpler: a bigger tap target and no hover state (just Idle, Pressed, Active, Disabled). Stack several inside a Mobile Dropdown to build the full menu.

When to use: for the items of a mobile menu, select, filter list, or calendar.
When not to use: on desktop (use Desktop Menu Options) or as a standalone button.

Types
• Combobox — a selectable option; the chosen one shows a trailing checkmark.
• Mobile Day Cell — a calendar day.
• Checkbox Selection / Radio Button Selection — rows with a built-in checkbox (pick several) or radio (pick one).

8338:35339.name: Mobile Dropdown
8338:35339.description:
The phone version of the floating menu panel: a white, rounded, bordered card with a soft shadow that stacks Mobile Menu Options rows and scrolls when the list is long. The mobile counterpart of Desktop Dropdowns, used by mobile selects and the mobile map search.

When to use: for any mobile overlay that lets the user pick from a list.
When not to use: for full-screen sheets or dialogs.

8230:26475.name: Desktop Dropdowns
8230:26475.description:
A ready-made floating panel that appears when you open a menu, select, filter, or picker — the white rounded card with a soft shadow that holds a list of Desktop Menu Options rows plus whatever extra pieces the variant needs (a search box, a pagination footer, an empty-state message, an account header, or footer buttons). Place it directly under the control that opens it.

When to use: for any overlay that lets the user pick or filter something.
When not to use: for full pages or confirmation dialogs (use a Modal), or for content shown inline on the page.

Variants
• Combobox — a scrollable single-select list with a pagination footer.
• Combobox-Search — the same list with a search box on top.
• Combobox-Search (Empty) — the search state when nothing matches.
• Combobox-Create — a list plus an 'Add "…"' action to create a new entry.
• Combobox-Create (Empty) — the create state when nothing matches, with a short hint.
• Actions Dropdown — a context / "⋯" menu of commands, ending in a red destructive action.
• User Menu — the account menu: avatar, name and email, then grouped links ending in Logout.
• Sort — pick a field to sort by (radio) and a direction (ascending / descending).
• Basic Filter — one checklist of filters; footer shows the result count + Close / Show Results.
• Filter Group — a two-pane filter: groups on the left, their searchable options on the right. The active group shows how many options are ticked; the footer shows the live result count with Reset / Show Results.
• Filter Group (Empty) — the same when the search finds no options.
• Timepicker — a scrollable list of times.
• Stacked List — a list of richer two-line rows (title + description) for results / entities.

7359:18098.name: File Upload Cards
7359:18098.description:
A File Upload Card is a single row of the file-upload queue (the File Upload Cards sub-instance of Data Entry). A muted card with a leading status glyph, filename and size, a trailing remove button, and — while uploading — a progress indicator. Uploading shows a Loading glyph + progress bar; Uploaded shows a Check-Circle glyph and no bar.

Usage: To show the status of a file being uploaded or already uploaded, within a File Upload field.
When not to use: the upload drop zone itself (use File Upload); generic content cards (use Content Card).

9894:18459.name: Option Cards
9894:18459.description:
What it is: A selectable card that presents a single option within a list of choices. Each card displays a title, description, and optional leading icon or illustration. Used inside pages and mobile trays to surface a list of options, actions, or features available to a user.

When to use: Feature selection screens, onboarding flows, settings panels where options are mutually exclusive or multi-select. Not for navigation (use Menu Options) or status display (use Badges/Tags).

9617:18100.name: Configuration Card
9617:18100.description:
What it is: A card surface for managing a single configurable setting or preference. Combines a Content Primitive (Utility or Group Header type) with an inline control (toggle, input, or selection) inside a bordered card container.

When to use: Settings pages, preference panels, admin configuration screens where each setting needs its own visual boundary. Not for bulk-edit forms (use a standard form layout) or read-only display (use a Dashboard Card or Content Primitive directly).

9896:25095.name: Content Card
9896:25095.description:
Content Card is a plain bordered surface that wraps a single content block — the generic card chrome you drop a Content Primitive into (white card, 1px neutral border, 12px corners, 20px padding, no shadow). Its Container slot holds any content; the default is a Utility primitive with a Dispatch action.

Usage: To present a content block (Utility / Metrics / List-Row / Progress primitive) on its own card surface.
When not to use: selectable choices (use Option Card); KPI tiles (use Metric Card); toggleable sections (use Configuration Card).

2794:28551.name: Stacked Cards
2794:28551.description:
Stacked Cards vertically stacks multiple Content Cards with a 16px gap. It has no surface of its own — it simply lays its children out in a column.

Usage: To present a list of Content Cards as a single group.
When not to use: a single card (use Content Card); tabular records (use Table / Data Rows).

6961:41406.name: Content Primitives
6961:41406.description:
What it is: A flexible content-layout primitive that standardises how headings, metadata, and trailing actions are arranged across the product. It is the foundational building block composed inside cards, modals, accordions, drawers, and list rows.

Variants (Type):
• Page Heading — top-level page title with optional subtext and trailing actions.
• Accordion Heading — single-line collapsible section header with trailing passive/interactive elements.
• Group Header — section sub-heading within a page or card; supports a Visual Anchor (icon, avatar, featured icon, or monogram).
• Utility — compact labelled row for settings and configuration; supports Visual Anchor and accepts Selection Controls, Segment Controls, or Text Fields as interactive elements.
• Progress Indicator — title + subtext row above a linear progress bar; trailing actions limited to Buttons.
• Vertical List Row — stacked title-over-description pair with optional leading icon and copy action.
• Horizontal List Row — side-by-side title and description with optional leading icon and copy action.
• Metrics — KPI display: leading icon, eyebrow label, large metric value, optional variance badge, and subtext.

Interactive element constraints: List Row types accept only a Button. Progress Indicator accepts only a Button. Utility accepts Text Field, Grouped Input, Selection Control (Toggle, Radio, Checkbox) & Segment Controls.

4239:74634.name: Desktop Metric Cards
4239:74634.description:
What it is: A card surface for displaying key performance indicators on dashboards. Wraps a Metrics-type Content Primitive inside a bordered card container with optional chart or sparkline visualisation.

When to use: Dashboard summary panels, KPI grids, analytics overviews. Not for inline metrics within forms or list rows (use the Metrics Content Primitive directly).

3811:65015.name: Notification Banners
3811:65015.description:
Neutral
Use for:
contextual information
passive guidance
operational notes
lightweight status
supporting information
non-urgent reminders
Examples:
“Comments are editable for 5 minutes”
“Driver location updates every 30 seconds”
“Changes save automatically”

InfoUse for:
actionable awareness
important feature-specific information
workflow-impacting context
things users should actively notice
Examples:
“This action affects all active orders”
“Tracking links expire after 24 hours”
“Bulk update is currently processing”


Highlight
Use for:
automation
smart suggestions
feature discovery
optimization opportunities
AI/system assistance
workflow acceleration
Examples:
“This order was automatically assigned”
“Try route optimization”
“Auto-scheduling can reduce delays”
“Recommended dispatch available”




WarningUse for:
Non-blocking issues
System disruptions
Impending limits
Irreversible actions
Data vulnerabilities
Conditional status changes

Examples:
“Your session expires in 2 minutes”
“Storage is 90% full”
“This action cannot be undone”
“API response time is currently slow”
“Changes will not save automatically”




ErrorUse for:
System failures
Critical blockades
Validation rejections
Data loss events
Network disconnections
Permission denials

Examples:
“Payment failed. Please try again”
“Connection lost. Reconnecting...”
“Invalid file format uploaded”
“Unable to save your changes”
“Access denied. Administrator permission required”

68:36772.name: Desktop Toast
68:36772.description:
A transient, auto-dismissing notification confirming the outcome of an action. Anchored to the bottom-right of the screen and persists for ~7 seconds before dismissing (or until dismissed). Types: Success (completed), Warning (cautionary), Error (failed). Optional title, subtitle, and CTA; always dismissable.

When to use: confirm the result of an action the user just took.
When NOT to use: persistent/in-context messages (use Notification Banner), blocking confirmations (use a dialog), or field-level validation (use the field's error/warning state).

7292:86007.name: Pagination
7292:86007.description:
What it is: Navigation control for moving through paged data sets, in the layout that fits its host surface.

When to use: Below tables, lists, or comboboxes with more results than fit one view. Not for step-through wizards (use Page Tabs Control / Stepper) or carousels (use Carousel Pagination).

Variants:
• Table — full data tables: page-number controls with prev/next, typically paired with a rows-per-page selector and result count.
• Combobox — compact, space-constrained contexts (inside a dropdown/combobox or toolbar): a condensed "page X of Y" selector with prev/next.
• Stacked List — stacked/list layouts (incl. narrow/mobile-leaning views): simplified prev/next (and/or load-more) sized for list rows.

6448:32008.name: Footer Frame
6448:32008.description:
What it is: The bottom action region of modals, cards, and panels — houses primary/secondary actions and contextual footer content, pinned below the body.

When to use: Whenever a surface needs committed actions or summary at its base. Not for inline actions within content.

Variants:
• Default — the standard primary + secondary action pair (e.g. Save / Cancel); right-aligned buttons.
• Validation Footer — forms that can fail validation: surfaces a validation/error message alongside the actions; primary may be disabled until valid.
• Data Summary — when the footer must show a running total/count next to actions (e.g. "3 items selected"): summary text on the leading edge, actions trailing.
• Tertiary Action — when a low-priority or destructive action (e.g. "Delete", "Reset") must sit apart from the main pair: adds a leading/plain tertiary action separated from primary/secondary.
• Preference Footer — footers carrying a persistent preference (e.g. a "Don't show again" toggle) with actions: control on the leading edge + actions trailing.
• Card Footer — compact footer for Cards (shorter height than modal footers): condensed actions sized for card density.

Scroll behaviour: when the footer pins below a SCROLLABLE region (a modal/panel/dropdown whose body overflows), a subtle upward drop shadow appears above the footer so it reads as separated from the content scrolling underneath. This applies to EVERY variant EXCEPT Card Footer — Card footers are flush/transparent and never show this shadow. (In code this is the FooterFrame `scrollShadow` prop; it's already wired into the Desktop Dropdowns Basic Filter / Filter Group footers.)

228:5568.name: Modal Headers
228:5568.description:
What it is: The top region of a modal/dialog — title, optional supporting text, and a close affordance — that anchors the modal and (optionally) hosts in-modal navigation.

When to use: At the top of every modal/dialog. Not for page-level headers (use Top Page Section) or drawers with their own chrome.

Variants:
• Default — standard single-view modals (confirmations, forms, detail panels): title + optional sub-text + close button; the body sits directly beneath.
• With Tabs — modals whose content is split into sections the user switches between without leaving the modal: adds a Page Tabs Control beneath the title; the active tab swaps the modal body.

37:362.name: Selection Control
37:362.description:
What it is: A unified, labelled selection-input wrapper that renders a Checkbox, Radio Button, or Switch with consistent label, spacing, and states. Composes the existing Toggle / Checkbox / Radio Button atoms.

When to use: Anywhere a labelled boolean/option control is needed in forms, lists, and settings. Not as a bare glyph (use the underlying atoms directly only when you supply your own label).

Variants (Variant × State):
• Checkbox — multi-select / independent on-off options. States: Idle, Active (checked), Indeterminate (partial / parent of mixed children), Disabled.
• Radio Button — single-select within a group (mutually exclusive). States: Idle, Active (selected), Disabled. (No Indeterminate.)
• Switch — immediate on/off toggles for a setting (no submit needed). States: Idle (off), Active (on), Disabled.

7353:37255.name: Desktop Progress Indicator
7353:37255.description:
What it is: A linear progress bar that communicates completion of an ongoing or measured process. (Distinct from the 14×14 Progress Tracker donut used in mobile badges.)

When to use: For determinate progress with a known percentage. Not for indeterminate spinners or tiny inline indicators.

Variants (Type × Status 0/50/100%):
• Upload — file/asset upload progress: fills as bytes transfer; pairs with filename/size; 100% = complete.
• Task — multi-step task or job completion (e.g. onboarding, processing): fills as steps complete.
• System Process — system-driven progress and background processing (e.g. "API usage: 60% of limit", "storage used", "task queue progress"): reflects a system/background process's completion against its limit or total.

7339:29150.name: Empty State
7339:29150.description:
A centred placeholder for a view, list, or container that has no content: illustration + heading + supporting copy + optional CTA (Container slot). Size: Large (full-page) / Small (cards, panels, results). Types are presets with a matching illustration + default copy: No Orders, No Results, No Drivers, No Trips, No Products, No Data, No Items, No Reviews, No Suspensions, No Network, New Update, Broadcast starting soon, File Upload, Image Upload, and No Results (No Icon).

When to use: first-run states, empty lists/tables, zero search results, offline placeholders, upload prompts.
When NOT to use: loading states (use a skeleton/spinner), inline form validation, or transient confirmations (use a Toast).

7259:81045.name: Shortcut
7259:81045.description:
What it is: A compact, contextual inline element placed at the trailing edge of dropdown menu items, command-palette rows, or list items. It surfaces the keyboard combination that triggers that action, reinforcing power-user workflows and discoverability.

When to use: Beside an actionable menu/command/list item that has a keyboard binding. Not for non-actionable text or where no shortcut exists.

Variants:
• Idle (Default / Active) — the shortcut is operational and available because its parent option is interactive: renders the key combination at full emphasis.
• Disabled — the parent option and its key binding are inactive: muted styling; communicates the shortcut can't be triggered right now.

7111:43945.name: Leading Input Field Element
7111:43945.description:
What it is: An interactive adornment that sits at the leading edge inside an input field. Current type: a Country Selector for phone/locale inputs.

When to use: Inside text/phone inputs that need a leading selector or context. Not as a standalone control outside an input.

Variants (Type: Country Selector × Variant × State):
• Single Country — inputs locked to one country (display-only flag + dial code): shows flag + code; non-selectable.
• Multiple Countries — inputs where the user picks the country: flag + code + chevron; opens a country picker. States: Idle, Hover, Pressed, Focus, Disabled.

9108:33624.name: Breadcrumbs
9108:33624.description:
What it is: A horizontal trail showing the user's location in a hierarchy, with each ancestor a link back up the tree.

When to use: On pages nested ≥2 levels deep so users can orient and navigate up. Not for flat hierarchies or as primary navigation.

Variants:
• Nested — in-page/section hierarchy within a product area: shows the path within the current app section.
• Global Client — top-level wayfinding in the client-facing app: roots the trail at the client app's home/context — the highest level a client user navigates from.
• Global Admin — top-level wayfinding in the admin app, used by an admin who has access to multiple client instances to switch between different "Client" profiles. The root-level label is a split icon button (its trailing icon is the design system's stepper); clicking it opens a dropdown of client instances the admin can switch between.

5146:845211.name: Grouped Input
5146:845211.description:
Grouped Input lays out several data-entry fields together as one unit. Three layouts: Multi-field — a row of input fields ending in an optional trailing element (e.g. a unit dropdown); Range input — two fields joined by a "→" arrow for a from→to range; Time input stepper — an editable label above a row of Hours / Minutes / Seconds stepper inputs.

Usage: When several fields form one logical value (a date range, a duration, an address row).
When not to use: a single field (use Input Field).

4524:205932.name: Stepper
4524:205932.description:
What it is: A numeric input with increment/decrement affordances for adjusting a value in discrete steps.

When to use: Bounded numeric entry where ± nudging is faster than typing (quantities, counts, durations). Not for unbounded numbers, ranges (use a slider), or step-through wizards.

Variants (Type × State):
• Discrete — a control for adjusting a single numeric value representing a count of identical items. Uses simple increment/decrement actions (+ / –) to add or remove units. Each step is uniform and independent. Best for quantities like item counts, participants, tickets, or any value where parts have no internal structure. States: Default, Hover, Focus, Disabled.
• Segmented — a control for adjusting a structured numeric value composed of multiple interdependent units. Each segment represents a bounded part of a larger value (e.g. hours, minutes, seconds, distance) with rules such as rollover and constrained ranges. Best for time, durations, and other compound values where each part has meaning within a system. States: Default, Active, Focus, Disabled.

4478:344281.name: Trailing Input Field Element
4478:344281.description:
What it is: An adornment that sits at the trailing edge inside an input field.

When to use: Inside inputs needing a trailing action or selector. Not standalone.

Variants:
• Basic — a simple trailing affordance (e.g. clear "✕", status icon, or unit text): presentational or single-action.
• Dropdown — inputs that open a selector from the trailing edge (e.g. unit/currency/timezone picker): chevron affordance that opens an overlay.

1182:17579.name: Date & Time Pickers
1182:17579.description:
Date & Time Pickers — pick a single date (Simple Date Picker), a date range (Date Range: a preset list — Today / Yesterday / Last 7 Days / Last 30 Days / Custom — beside dual month calendars), or a date and a time (Date & Time: a calendar beside a scrollable Time-of-Day list). Available on Desktop and Mobile. Desktop calendars show the abbreviated month ("Nov 2024"); mobile shows the full month. A selected range paints a continuous grey band across the in-between days, with the start and end days emphasised.

Footer (Card Footer with a Notification Banner on the left + Clear / Apply on the right):
• Date & Time — when no date/time is selected, or only the date, or only the time, the banner reads "Select date & time". Once BOTH a date and a time are selected, the banner shows the chosen value, e.g. "Nov 5 2024, 12:30 AM".
• Date Range — the banner reads "{N} days selected".
• Apply is disabled until a valid selection is complete.

1178:15588.name: Tooltips & Popovers
1178:15588.description:
A contextual overlay that surfaces supplementary information or guidance, anchored to a target element with a directional caret. Variants: Small (a compact label/hint), Standard (a short descriptive paragraph), Coachmark (an onboarding step — title, body, "1 of N" counter, Skip/Next), Flyout (title + body + confirm/cancel actions), and Advanced (title + a grid of detail rows + optional controls + a primary action). The caret can point from any edge (top/bottom × left/middle/right) toward the anchor. Type: Desktop / Mobile.

When to use: explain an icon/control, guide first-time users (Coachmark), or surface quick confirmations/detail peeks (Flyout/Advanced).
When NOT to use: persistent page content, critical messages that must not dismiss on blur (use a dialog), or long-form content.

38:42.name: Data Entry
38:42.description:
A labelled text-entry field — the primary control for collecting typed or selected input in forms. Composes a label (with optional required/optional tag and helper icon), a field row, and a helper/error/warning line with an optional character counter. Variants (field-row composition): Basic, Leading Element (leading selector, e.g. country/unit), Trailing Element (trailing selector/unit), Selection (dropdown trigger), Stepper (numeric +/-). States (Idle/Active/Focus/Warning/Error/Disabled) are runtime, driven by focus + error/warning/disabled.

PLANNED RESTRUCTURE: Search Bar, Text Area, and File Upload will be split into their own sibling components so this set covers only the core Input Field.

When to use: any form input — text, numbers, selections, phone numbers, currency.
When NOT to use: free-form multi-line text (use Text Area), full-text search (use Search Bar), or file attachment (use File Upload).

7519:27364.name: Top Bar
7519:27364.description:
Description
A top bar is a persistent application-level component positioned at the top of the interface. It provides access to global actions, system notifications, user account controls, and contextual navigation. The top bar remains visible across the application, allowing users to manage their account, review notifications, and understand their location within the product through breadcrumbs.
Usage Guidelines
Use for controls and information that should be accessible throughout the application.
Display breadcrumbs to help users understand their current location and navigate back to previous levels.
Provide access to user account actions and preferences.
Surface notifications and system updates that may require user attention.
Maintain a consistent layout across all application screens.
Avoid placing page-specific navigation or actions that are only relevant to a single screen.

7751:26369.name: Sidetab
7751:26369.description:
Description
A side tab is a secondary navigation component used within a single module, page, or workflow. It helps users move between related sections without leaving the current context. Side tabs organize complex content into logical groups and expose page-level navigation rather than product-level navigation.
Usage Guidelines
Use for navigation within a module or page.
All tabs should belong to the same parent context.
Selecting a tab changes the visible content while keeping the user in the same workflow.
Side tabs should not be used to navigate between unrelated product areas.
Labels should clearly describe the section they reveal.
The active state indicates the currently displayed section.

3714:35743.name: Side Bar
3714:35743.description:
Description
A sidebar is the primary navigation component used to move between major product areas and workflows. It provides persistent access to top-level destinations and remains available across most application screens. Users rely on the sidebar to switch contexts within the platform rather than navigate within a specific page.
Usage Guidelines
Use for top-level navigation only.
Each item should represent a distinct product area, module, or workflow.
The active state indicates the user's current location within the application.
The sidebar should remain consistent across the product to support orientation and wayfinding.
Avoid using sidebar items for actions, settings subsections, filters, or page-level navigation.
May support nested navigation when a module contains multiple related sections.
Common Content
Dashboard
Orders
Deliveries
Drivers
Fleet
Reports
Settings
Support

7973:17061.name: Bulk Actions Toolbar
7973:17061.description:
Bulk Actions Toolbar — a floating pill overlay that appears when one or more table rows are selected.

What it is: a fixed-position horizontal pill (white surface, shadow-neutral-3, rounding-xl) with three zones separated by vertical dividers: a left selection control + optional summary, a centre action-button group (Ghost / Ghost-Error leading-icon buttons + a "More" overflow), and a right dismiss ×. Disappears when the selection is cleared.

Selection control: a Ghost button labelled "N selected" with a TRAILING Chevron-Down icon ("10 selected ⌄") — a dropdown affordance for selection-scope options, not a leading checkbox. An optional summary (e.g. "800 kg · 980 cases") renders to its right with the value SemiBold and the unit Regular.

Icon convention — OUTLINED BY DEFAULT: every icon-bearing button in this toolbar (the default actions and any actions you supply) should use the outlined variant of the library icon rather than its filled variant; the filled variant is only a fallback for glyphs that have no outline (e.g. Proceed, More, Cancel). In React this is the Button `iconOutlined` prop. Defaulting to outlined icons guarantees the toolbar renders correctly on every screen it is dropped into.

When to use: after the user selects rows in a Table, to surface batch operations (export, delete, assign, etc.).
When not to use: for single-row actions — use the Cell's actions column instead. For page-level primary actions, use the Table Data Control toolbar.

React: `BulkActionsToolbar` from `@leta/components`. Key props:
- `selectionLabel` — text shown in the selection control (default "10 selected")
- `summaryItems[]` — optional weight/volume metadata ({ value, unit })
- `actions` — the action Buttons rendered in the centre zone (pass `iconOutlined` on each)
- `onSelectionClick` / `onOverflowClick` / `onClose` — handlers
- `fixed` — apply the real placement (fixed, centred, 80px from the viewport bottom)

2192:8107.name: Table
2192:8107.description:
Table — a bordered data-table card: a sticky header row, a scrollable body of Data Rows, and a Pagination footer.

**What it is:** a white card (surface-neutral-bg-default, 1px border, rounding-xl) with three vertical regions: a fixed header Cell row (column labels, optional select-all checkbox), a scrollable body area (Data Rows — capped by `maxBodyHeight` for sticky scroll), and a Pagination footer with row-count selector.

**When to use:** as the primary data-grid surface inside a Table Container or Table Section. Pass `columns` and `rows` for data-driven rendering; use `selectable` for row-checkbox selection.

**When not to use:** for read-only key-value information — use List Section instead. For a standalone info card, use Content Card.

**Key props:**
- `columns[]` — column definitions; each carries `role` / `flex` / `minWidth` / `maxWidth` / `accessibleName` (see Column layout below)
- `rows[]` — row data (one array of cell values per row)
- `selectable` — adds the 52px select-all header checkbox + per-row checkboxes
- `maxBodyHeight` — caps body height and activates sticky scroll

—————

## Column layout (Table Column Layout Specification)

A **System** (rules every table obeys) + **Instances** (individual tables). Adding a table = a new instance/preset, not a redesign.

**Never make all columns equal width** — widths reflect the importance and variability of content, optimizing for information density, not symmetry.

**Classify every column:**
• Primary (Driver, Route, Recipient, Customer) — FLEXIBLE: shares remaining width by weight. Never fixed.
• Primary Identifier (Order ID, Trip ID) — FIXED; truncates safely. May be promoted to BOUNDED-FLEXIBLE (a min–max band + low weight) for balance — never unbounded.
• Secondary (Service Type, Region, and Depot when there's no Route cell) — FIXED.
• Utility (Status, Duration, Created) — FIXED; only as wide as content needs.
• Control (Checkbox, Overflow, Actions, Dispatch) — FIXED, smallest; NO visible header, but the header cell keeps a screen-reader-only accessible name (e.g. "Actions") so it's never announced as empty.

**Width model:** sum the fixed columns, then the primary columns share ALL remaining width by weight. Weights are the flexible-share ratio with Recipient = 1.00: Route 1.48 (40%) · Driver 1.22 (33%) · Recipient 1.00 (27%). Every flexible column has a min-width FLOOR (p90 name length, not the longest possible). Order ID is bounded-flexible: min 150 · max 224 · low weight (past 224 the surplus goes to Route). Route is a COMPOSITE cell (depot + pickup + drop-off stacked) — so Depot is NOT its own column in the Order table.

**Allocation:** give fixed columns their width → satisfy every floor (incl. the bounded-flex min) → distribute surplus among primary columns by weight; grow the bounded column within its band. On shrink, a column freezes at its floor and the remaining shrink redistributes to columns still above their floors (Recipient — full phone, smallest share — freezes first). Below all floors, the instance either drops columns or switches to horizontal scroll (declared per instance).

**Two priority lists:** width-allocation (who gets surplus) Route → Driver → Recipient → Order ID; column-drop (who hides first) Secondary, then lower Utility — NEVER drop Status, Checkbox, or Actions.

**Truncation:** Route — keep enough of each line to distinguish locations. Driver — always show avatar + name; phone is a call button, not text. Recipient — always show name; phone must not truncate. Order ID — ~18–24 chars then ellipsis; full value via copy/tooltip/detail.

**Interaction:** Static tables (Logs) are read-only — no hover/pressed. Dynamic tables (Orders) — the row opens the record drawer (hover + pressed); the checkbox is the ONLY selection target; inline cell buttons stop propagation so a mis-aimed tap never opens the drawer.

**Instances:** columns drop progressively by lifecycle stage. A · Order Table (active — Assigned/Dispatched onward) — the full set: Fixed Checkbox 52 · Trip 90 · Duration 110 · Created 120 · Status 140 · Actions 154; Flexible Route/Driver/Recipient; Bounded Order ID 150–224; Last Updated (120) hidden by default (column-control toggle). C · Unassigned (scheduled/pending/broadcasted — no driver or trip assigned yet) — Order Table minus Driver AND Trip; Driver's share redistributes proportionally → Route 60 / Recipient 40 (automatic, not a manual re-split); Duration stays. B · Scheduled (unassigned AND pre-queue) — the Unassigned set minus Duration (no SLA until Pending): no Driver, no Trip, no Duration. D · User Management (illustrative) — no Route cell, so Depot is its own Secondary column.

**React:** `TableColumn.role` ('primary' | 'identifier' | 'secondary' | 'utility' | 'control') defaults the sizing mode; `flex` = weight (Recipient = 1.00 base); `minWidth` = floor; `maxWidth` = bounded-flex cap; `accessibleName` = SR-only header for no-label columns. Reuse the `ORDER_TABLE_COLUMNS` / `SCHEDULED_ORDER_COLUMNS` / `UNASSIGNED_ORDER_COLUMNS` / `LAST_UPDATED_COLUMN` presets from @leta/components; the checkbox column comes from `selectable`.

7575:36637.name: Table Data Control
7575:36637.description:
The toolbar row that sits above a data table to support search, filtering, sorting, and row-level actions. Five variants cover the common table header patterns:

• Search + Create — search/filter controls on the left, primary Add Order + Import/Export CTAs on the right.
• Search + Create (Active Filter) — same layout, but with one or more active filter rules applied: the Filter button condenses to a 40×40 icon-only control with a Coral Red (Alt) count badge at the top-right corner, and Sort By condenses to icon-only.
• Search + Column Control — search/filter on the left, row count + Columns control on the right. The Columns control is a Secondary icon-only button (outlined Columns glyph, no text label).
• Search + Column Control (Active) — same layout with the active-filter treatment described above.
• Top Filters + Column Control — a Top Filter Section chip-bar on the left, row count + Columns on the right.

React: `TableDataControl` from `@leta/components`. The left and right sections are slot props with Figma-faithful defaults. Pass `filterCount` (number of active rules) to trigger the active-filter state on the search variants — set it back to 0 to restore the labelled Filter + Sort By buttons.

When to use: as the controls row of a Table Container.
When not to use: bulk-row actions (use Bulk Actions Toolbar); in-table pagination (use the Table's Pagination footer).

4445:105200.name: Data Rows
4445:105200.description:
Data Rows — a flush row group that stacks individual Cell rows inside a Table body.

**What it is:** a vertical container of Cell rows sharing the same column widths, with no outer border or surface. Each row renders one of the 18 Cell types (header, text, status, date, checkbox, actions, etc.) and can be idle, hovered, pressed, or selected.

**When to use:** as the body of a Table component. Pass rows via the data-driven `cells[]` array or inject arbitrary rows via `children`.

**When not to use:** as a standalone information list — use List Section instead. For a single row, use Cell directly.

**Key props:**
- `cells[]` — column definitions shared across rows (type, state, width)
- `selectable` — enables per-row checkbox selection
- `state` — forces a visual state on every row (idle / hover / pressed / selected) for catalog stories

1524:40283.name: Table-Container
1524:40283.description:
Table Container — the full data-table section: a vertical stack of Table Data Control toolbar(s) and either a populated Table or a centred Empty State.

**What it is:** a width-filling vertical stack (gap 16) with two variants:
- **Default** — two Table Data Control rows (search-create + filters-column) above a Table.
- **Empty State** — a single search-create control above a centred "No Orders Yet" Empty State with an Add Order CTA.

All regions are slot props with Figma-faithful defaults; pass your own Table, controls, or empty content to customise.

**When to use:** as the top-level shell for any table view (toolbar + table + empty state handling).

**When not to use:** just the grid alone (use Table) or just the toolbar (use Table Data Control). For a titled, grouped table, wrap this in Table Section.

**Key props:**
- `variant` — `default` | `empty`
- `controls` — override the default toolbar row(s)
- `table` — override the default Table
- `empty` — override the default Empty State

7900:165088.name: Extra Large Modal
7900:165088.description:
Extra Large Modal — a 1024×768 two-panel workspace for selecting and managing records against a map. A left data panel (a section heading, a Search / Filter / Sort toolbar, and a selectable table) sits beside a live, interactive map panel; the footer carries Cancel / Confirm. Used to preview objects of interest on a map alongside a panel for viewing and managing the underlying data — the left panel can be collapsed to reveal more of the map.

How it works: the table keeps its columns at a fixed width and scrolls horizontally (and vertically) rather than shrinking cells; the map pans and zooms, with a zoom control bottom-right.

When to use: assigning, routing, or reviewing records that have a geographic dimension — where the data table and the map need to be seen side by side.
When NOT to use: data with no map relevance (use a page-level Table or a smaller modal), or a simple form / confirmation (use Modal / Alert Dialog).

Scrolling: the Footer Frame no longer has a top outline. When the main body overflows, a subtle drop shadow appears — under the header once it has been scrolled away from the top, and above the footer while more content sits below — to signal that the body is scrollable (each shadow hides at its respective scroll end).

1317:4855.name: Modal Dialog
1317:4855.description:
Modal Dialog — a 512-wide single-purpose dialog for one focused task: capturing a short input or a small form, previewing a signature or image, or picking from a set of choices. A title header, a task-specific body, and a Close / Confirm footer.

Types:
• Comment — a single multi-line Text Area (with character counter).
• Form — a compact two-column row of Input Fields.
• Signature — a bordered preview of a captured signature.
• Image — an image preview (e.g. proof of delivery).
• Multi-choice — a scrollable list of selectable Option Cards.

When to use: a single, self-contained task — capturing a note or a couple of fields, previewing a signature or image, or picking from a set.
When NOT to use: a simple yes/no confirmation (use Alert Dialog), or a long / multi-section form or a map-and-table workflow (use Large / Extra Large Modal).

Scrolling: the Footer Frame no longer has a top outline. When the main body overflows, a subtle drop shadow appears — under the header once it has been scrolled away from the top, and above the footer while more content sits below — to signal that the body is scrollable (each shadow hides at its respective scroll end).

1106:2868.name: Alert Dialog
1106:2868.description:
Alert Dialog — the smallest modal: a focused confirmation or destructive-action prompt. A compact 480-wide dialog with a title, a short body message, and two footer actions (a secondary Close + a primary Confirm).

Variants:
• Basic — title + message.
• Warning — adds a leading warning icon beside the title for cautionary or destructive confirmations.

When to use: confirm a single, discrete action — especially a destructive or irreversible one (delete, discard, proceed).
When NOT to use: anything that needs input fields or multiple steps (use Modal Dialog), or content-heavy / two-panel flows (use Large / Extra Large Modal). To confirm a completed action non-blockingly, use a Toast.

Scrolling: the Footer Frame no longer has a top outline. When the main body overflows, a subtle drop shadow appears — under the header once it has been scrolled away from the top, and above the footer while more content sits below — to signal that the body is scrollable (each shadow hides at its respective scroll end).

228:11413.name: Large Modal
228:11413.description:
Large Modal — a 768-wide modal for substantial, multi-section content. It comes as full-height side-sheet drawers or a centered dialog; the header and footer stay fixed (sticky) while the body scrolls, and the footer is a preference footer (a "Don't show this again" control on the left + Close / Confirm).

Variants:
• Single Column Form — a full-viewport-height side sheet with a tabbed header over one vertical column of Input Sections. Use for SHORT forms where the user doesn't need to work through 4 or more accordions nesting text fields and the like.
• Dual Column Form — the same side sheet, but the form is split into two columns separated by a vertical demarcator. Use when the form is long enough that a single column means heavy scrolling and the fields fall into 2+ independent groups.
• Read — the side sheet showing read-only List Sections; for reviewing a record's details.
• Centered — a rounded 768×768 dialog pairing an Input Section with a Table Section; for a configuration step that combines a short form with tabular data.

When to use: creating, editing, or reviewing a record with several grouped sections.
When NOT to use: a single field or short modal task (use Modal Dialog), a simple confirmation (use Alert Dialog), or a map-based picker (use Extra Large Modal).

Scrolling: the Footer Frame no longer has a top outline. When the main body overflows, a subtle drop shadow appears — under the header once it has been scrolled away from the top, and above the footer while more content sits below — to signal that the body is scrollable (each shadow hides at its respective scroll end).

9626:14490.name: Page
9626:14490.description:
Page — a full desktop application screen: a collapsed Side Bar rail beside a viewport of a Top Bar and a scrolling body. The body opens with a page Title and a Page Tabs Control, then an info Notification Banner, then the screen's main content.

Variants:
• Data Table — a Table Container (search / filter toolbar + table + pagination); for browsing and acting on a set of records.
• Configuration — a Sidetab nav beside a column of Configuration Cards; for grouped settings and toggleable sections.

The Side Bar and Top Bar stay fixed; only the body scrolls. Composition only — every region is an already-built organism or molecule.

When to use: the top-level shell for a product screen.
When NOT to use: a modal / dialog flow (use the Templates), or a single component in isolation.
